class="css-uzyn7p"><div class="css-1vxywau"><p class="css-1nng8z9">transcript</p><h2 class="css-8p0vwh">Disney’s Former C.E.O. Gave Me the Exit Interview I Asked for</h2><h4 class="css-qsd3hm">The recently retired Disney executive talks about how Star Wars helped Disney survive the streaming wars and why NFTs are here to stay.</h4><time datetime="2022-01-27T10:00:08.000Z" class="css-1e6es05">2022-01-27T05:00:08-05:00</time></div><dl class="css-p98d0w"><dt class="css-xx7kwh"></dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">(SINGING) When you walk in the room, do you have sway?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I’m Kara Swisher, and you’re listening to “Sway“. With four kids, I’ve watched a lot of Disney films over the years. The latest, “Encanto“, is already a huge hit with my two-year-old, and that “Bruno” song is seared forever in my head. No, no, no.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">archived recording</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">(SINGING) No, no, no, no, we don’t talk about Bruno.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Who’s to blame for that? In part, it’s former Disney C.E..O., Bob Iger. Iger’s been in the entertainment business for nearly 50 years. He ran Disney for almost 15, during which time he oversaw an astonishing array of deals— among them, the purchase of Pixar, Marvel Studios, Lucasfilm, and various T.V. and film assets from Fox.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">That means Buzz Lightyear, Captain Marvel, Luke Skywalker, and—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">archived recording</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">D’oh!</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">—“The Simpsons.” He also oversaw the launch of the streaming platform, Disney Plus, which now has over 100 million subscribers. Iger became almost synonymous with Disney, so it was big news when he announced in 2020 that he would step down as C.E.O. that year, and would give up his chairman’s seat at the end of 2021.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">So now that he’s a few weeks into his retirement, I sat down with Iger to ask him how he led an almost 100-year-old studio through the streaming wars, and find out where he thinks Hollywood is going next. We taped this conversation in front of a live audience at the Richmond Forum.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">Wow. Look, Bob, people. I see people, live people.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">No, we’re actually in the metaverse.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Oh, we are. Cool. That’s great. There’s very few people, I was saying, that I would put on pants for, because I’ve been living in sweatpants for the past two years. So I’m very excited to be here in pants with you. So let’s just get started. Let’s first talk about your retirement. So it’s three weeks?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">It was three weeks ago, yes. It’s very different.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Is it?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">First of all, I have to wait on lines at theme park attractions.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK. All right.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I don’t wear a Bob name tag anymore.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">And I joked earlier, I don’t tell my wife to have a magical day every morning.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK, all right.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">So this is—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I’m amazed you’ve been married this long if you say that to her every morning. But three weeks, so three weeks. So—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I’m a rookie at retirement.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">You finally retired from Disney. It took four times, I think, right? You were going to retire, and then not retire. I was always almost writing, Bob Iger’s leaving Disney, and then you didn’t. Why did you do that now? What was the thinking?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I think, by the way, flunking retirement four times is, I think, always just a little bit inflated.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">But I always wanted to leave when I felt like I still had it in me to do more, but that things were good and I didn’t want to tempt fate. And I also didn’t want to get to a point where people at the company were saying, when is he finally going to go. And there are couple of other factors.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">One, I think change at the top has value. Bringing someone in with a fresh perspective is like opening the windows and letting fresh air blow in. Secondly, I was starting to get a little bit arrogant and a little bit overconfident in my own instincts. And what I mean by that is, I was sensing I was becoming a little bit more impatient or a little bit more intolerable, I should say, of other people’s ideas. I think because subconsciously, I felt like I was always right, or I knew it all.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Mm-hmm.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">And things had been quite good at the company in the period of time that I was C.E.O. And so I think that resulted in my believing in my own instincts so much, that I was becoming a little bit less open to other people’s. Anyway, I felt it was time.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Right. But one of the things— CNBC polled 10 media executives anonymously about their 2022 predictions, and one was that you’ll return to Disney.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">As what?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I don’t know. A Mickey Mouse character.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I would love that. Yes. First of all, you know, I was a weatherman when I started my career in 1973. And somewhat impishly, I said to people, before I leave Disney, I’d like to do the weather one more time. We own T.V. stations in a variety of cities.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">So the head of our station in L.A. said, well, you can come do the weather. So the last week I was at Disney or two weeks before, I did the weather. And then, it stays on that platform sometimes forever, meaning it doesn’t have any other life. So the revenue that is associated with it, one, is very different in terms of where it comes from. But two, it’s not like there’s direct revenue attributed to that film.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Right.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">So the whole industry right now is struggling to contend with how people are paid in this new world order. And obviously, that created tension.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">It was more than tension. Did you have a choice?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Well, a choice in what sense?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">In terms of not going to streaming. You’d be in the business of making money doing it, and you’re going to follow the money, which in this case is following the consumer. And so you don’t really have a choice if you want to stay in the business or grow the business, except to go in the streaming direction. And I made the decision for Disney to do that in 2015.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">We ultimately launched Disney Plus in 2019. So I don’t think there was a choice. Was there a choice in selecting films? So if you’re a writer, a director—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">So there’s more work is what you’re saying.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Much more.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">The streaming model— when you think about where streaming is going, is that the death of movie theaters? I think it’s a severe injury—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">—that maybe doesn’t heal.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">And what I mean by that is—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Wow.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Not fatal.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">It might be fatal to some. Let’s start with the movie-going experience, to say something good about that.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I think that people will still want to go to movies.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">However, they will be much more, I think, discerning about what movies they want to see out of the home, where you’re likely, I think, to say or ask yourself, wait a minute, is this a movie I need to see on the big screen and do all that, or can I wait or not even wait, for that matter, and see it at home?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Right.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">You know, “Spider-Man,” which Marvel and Walt Disney Company produced for Sony, when that came out there was a slew of people around the world wanting to see it first weekend. You can say, “my ex” if you want.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">You know, I was joking— Could do that. If you were to look at some of the films, some of the T.V. shows that Disney has made— “Mandalorian” being one, the Marvel series, “Falcon and the Winter Solider,” “Loki”— each one of those is a movie.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah. They were.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">So just, movie theaters have much more competition than they ever had before.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">So I just interviewed Joseph Gordon-Levitt on “Sway,” and he told me that Silicon Valley now owns Hollywood. But there’s no question that deep-pocketed technology companies— Apple being a great example of that, Amazon being another— have figured out that if they make intellectual property or tell stories, that it will benefit their other businesses— in Amazon’s case, to sell more Prime customers, in Apple’s case, I imagine, to sell more devices.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">And so they figured out that they can make TV and movies. Took them a little time, but not too long, to figure out how to make really good ones. “Ted Lasso” is a great example of a really fine show that they’ve made. Nothing to do with me. I—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">No.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I’m like all of you on that one. But the trouble is, competing with them is hard, because— I don’t want to suggest those are lost-leader businesses, but—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">They are lost-leader busi—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">But they’re in those businesses for other reasons.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Nonetheless, they have so much money. Like, the money they have is— this is a rounding error.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah, look. I can only speak for Disney. We view them, all of them, as competitors, but we never worried that they were going to put us out of business or own Hollywood, particularly since— and maybe this was a conscious decision that we made— actually, I made, when I became C.E.O.— is that if we own a lot of high-quality branded content, then—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Like Marvel or—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Marvel, Pixar, “Star Wars,” “The Simpsons,” “Avatar,” you name it— National Geographic— that that would enable us to withstand not only disruption of a business, but the incursion of new entrants into the business, even very, very deep-pocketed, very competitive competitors.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Because you had all this—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Because they can’t make a “Star Wars” movie. But you think about all the intellectual property. Marvel has 7,000 characters.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">And when that red Marvel comes on, which was purposeful in our part when we bought them, which has put a spotlight on the brand, it means something to the audience. Basically, the storytelling over multiple genres and multiple media has real value, and they don’t have that.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yet.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Well, maybe that’s one of the reasons why I retired. I don’t know.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah, really. Right.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">Because one of the things I was thinking— I remember talking to David Zaslav, who’s now going to be leading the combined Warner Discovery. One of the things I kept thinking is, you’re too small. Are these media companies too small, when you’re looking at what’s arrayed against you?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah. Those— these media companies— I’m going to leave Disney out.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Because Disney is not too small. But when Rupert Murdoch called me in 2017 to talk about possibly buying some of the assets of his company, he asked me whether I thought they were too small, and could they basically survive all of this new competition. And he was thinking— I think, smartly— of exiting a good part of the business, because he worried that they didn’t have the scale.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">I was thinking at the time, knowing that we were going to be launching Disney Plus and going into the streaming business, that if we had, on top of Marvel, Pixar, “Star Wars,” Disney, ESPN, ABC— if we had National Geographic, and “The Simpsons,” and “Avatar,” and the whole Fox library, and the great people talent that came with those assets, that we would have the scale, particularly to go into the streaming business.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Who does it? The other companies— I just read a story about Comcast not having enough, or— I do think Warner Discovery is too small.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I’m in an enviable position right now, just because I’m not working for Disney. Go ahead.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">But I’m going to choose not to single anybody out.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Oh, come on.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I think scale is an issue for a lot of the traditional companies that were in the television and movie business.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK. Well, that’s all of them then. OK. So do you need—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">That’s your interpretation of what I said.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">But it’s correct.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">But do you need to own a platform when you think about owning platforms? Is that critically important?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yes.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">—around the whole idea? And I mean that in a broader sense.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Disney was licensing movies to Netflix.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yes.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">And they were building— helping to build their platform—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yes, you did.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">—on the back of our movies, and having the direct relationship with the consumer and building this global subscription business, which they did a brilliant job of, really. And I woke up one day and thought, we’re basically selling nuclear weapons technology to a Third World country, and now they’re using it against us.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">That’s literally what I said.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Mm-hmm.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">So we decided at the time that we would stop licensing to Netflix and do it ourselves. And it resulted in a substantial decrease in our revenue, because we weaned ourselves off all that licensing—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Right, because it’s like, oh, look, money.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">But it thrust us into a business that is the most compelling growth engine in the media today.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Right, for Disney and for the rest of them. So when you think about this many streaming platforms— I know I pay for all of them right now.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Why?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I don’t know. My children do it. I— they just keep clicking. And that’s the end of my— that’s why I’m here, talking to you. Anyway—</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">So can there be this many? Can there be this many platforms?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I think it’s still too early to tell.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Does it become bundled?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Well, Disney’s bundling Hulu and ESPN Plus.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">But you own a big chunk of it.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah. I don’t know. I think it’s too early to tell. Everybody is trying. I don’t think they’ll all succeed. So the answer is probably no.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Probably no. Is there anyone you’d like to call out from— at the bottom of the pile?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Change the subject.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK. Paramount Plus. OK. Although they had “Yellowstone,” and they gave it over to Peacock. What’s going on with that?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I don’t know.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">By the way, watch “Yellowstone.” One of the things I’m thinking of is, recently, Microsoft announced buying Activision for $70 billion. Activision has obviously had some cultural issues, but lots of these gaming companies do. But can you talk a little bit about why that wasn’t bought by an entertainment company? That was sort of striking, that it was a tech company that bought it.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Well, first of all—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Now, Microsoft’s in gaming, obviously.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">It’s a big acquisition, in terms of money crossed.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yes.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">And going back to what you were suggesting about the entertainment companies not having the scale—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah, they don’t have the money.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">—they’re not necessarily on solid ground, from a financial perspective, and I don’t think that they could have easily done it. Now, Comcast’s big enough to do that. But I don’t think, in most cases, there were synergies to be gained from an entertainment company buying those.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">In Microsoft’s case— and I’m an outsider looking at this— clearly, they’re already in the gaming space—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Mm-hmm.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">—both on the platform side, and with games. So I think that it’s a natural fit. And then, gaming and playing games could be a very, very large component for the consumer of an internet 3.0, metaverse experience.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">So do entertainment companies need to be here then?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">It’s really hard. You can spill a lot of money very quickly—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">—getting into that. Need to be? No. Entertainment companies— to survive in an internet 3.0 world, you need to have some really compelling intellectual property.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">To turn into metaverse. We’re going to get that in a second, but I want to talk about two things— data, and then Steve Jobs, a little bit in terms of what you learned from him. But at your final Disney board retreat, you said, in a world of business that is awash in data, it seems tempting to use data as an answer to all our questions, including creative questions. I urge you all not to do that.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">At the same time, the person who took over after you, Bob Chapek, said, Disney is now a data-driven company. What is entertainment now? Is it data, or is it creativity?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I won’t comment about what Bob said, but what you’re quoting me as having said, which is accurate— I was attending what was our last— the last Disney board retreat that I would be at. And I opened the meeting thinking that I would leave them with some advice—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Right.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">—on how to go forward. And having spent almost 50 years on the creative side of our business, it became more and more clear to me that while data was already playing a very important role, that it should not be used to determine what stories are told. If we had tried to mine all the data that we had at the time to determine whether we should make a superhero movie that was essentially about an Afrofuturistic world with a Black cast, the data probably would have said, don’t do that.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Mm-hmm.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">And “Black Panther” never would have been made. There are a number of examples of that, where someone’s instinct or a group of people’s instinct on whether a story is worthy of being told and is in the hands of people who will tell it really well— I don’t think a machine or data, no matter how much technology enables, essentially, input of massive amounts of information to be processed— I don’t think you get the right answers to that.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">[APPLAUSE]</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">Thank you. And again, I think it’s valuable. Once you make something, it’s really valuable to learn about what people liked about it, what they didn’t like, should it continue. Interestingly enough, very effective in how to market.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">There’s that famous quote— which, I don’t know whether it’s apocryphal or not— about Henry Ford. And he said, if he had been asked what people wanted, they would have said, a faster horse—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">—instead of a car.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">To me, it is somewhat analogous, too. You need human beings to make those decisions.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">So talk a little bit about Steve Jobs, because one of the people who did like stories is Steve Jobs, compared to a lot— I mean, I’m sure you’ve met a million tech people, and they’re always telling you how they can figure everything out via technology. But Steve was quite interested in narrative and storytelling. He did that with the products of Apple and stuff. So talk a little bit about what you— that was one of your most significant, important relationships as a C.E.O., which really did—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">As a person, too. Actually, we became very close friends. First of all, Steve respected storytellers and respected the art of storytelling, knowing that a great story, really well told, was a miracle in many ways, that it wasn’t just an accident. And I think a lot of people in the tech world, because they’ve been so tech-centric, have not had the time in their lives or the experience to understand the intricacies of storytelling, the beauty of it, and really, the magic of it all.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">And when Steve bought Pixar from George Lucas, he immediately became immersed in real storytelling. And he was already a storyteller at heart. Just look at the marketing that he used to tell stories about Apple product. But this opened his eyes in a big way to the power of great stories.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">So when we bought Pixar from him and he became the largest shareholder of Disney— and then, ultimately, I ended up replacing Steve on the Apple board. But Steve knew that not only that there’s a miracle to great storytelling, but that the combination of using wonderful technology to both tell better stories— what Pixar did, just using technology, richer pictures and—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Or Lucas.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Or Lucas. But then, technology could be used to not only distribute it more effectively, but to consume it that way. So I think he was very, very unique, in terms of all the people I’ve met in the tech business. I’ve never met anyone that comes close to that understanding and the comprehension he has.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Possibly George Lucas, right, which you bought that company.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yes, I think George Lucas was a storyteller first that understood the power of technology in the storytellers’ hands. Walt Disney was another, by the way. Walt Disney once said that no matter what happens, in terms of the advances in technology it will improve or expand the purview of the storyteller.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Mm-hmm.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">So he was thinking that he could tell better stories using the tools of technology, which is what George did, and it’s what Steve did when he bought Pixar. And when I think about Steve, one of the things I miss the most is engaging with a person who is such a brilliant technologist that really got the other side— a left-right brain, and an extraordinary—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Do you imagine the technology— when I’m thinking about Lucas, years ago, we were doing an interview, and I said— he was talking about “Star Wars” and anything else he creates. And I said, someday, everything’s going to be distributed over this streaming, and I went on and on about this. This was about 10 years ago. And I said, do you need studios, I guess, or do you need—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Oh, I see. Well, studios can provide an important function. That’s money, of course, and— because they understand the process is somewhat messy. And sometimes if the studio executive is really good, they can add perspective and actually help a creator see things that the creator might not see because they’re so close to them.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">And sometimes it’s also marketing and platform and brand. So you know, if you’re a storyteller and you want to tell a story about superheroes, you’re better off telling it with Marvel superheroes, distributed by Disney, than you are with— Apple doesn’t have any superheroes— creating one Kara Swisher superhero, and—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">They should.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">—good luck.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK. It’s going to be fantastic. It’s going to be so good.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">[MUSIC PLAYING]</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">We’ll be back in a minute. If you like this interview and want to hear others, follow us on your favorite podcast app. You’ll be able to catch up on “Sway” episodes you may have missed, like my conversation with Jeffrey Katzenberg, and you’ll get new ones delivered directly to you. More with Bob Iger after the break.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">There is a call to break up tech companies, that they’re too powerful. Talk about what you think of the current situation right now, looking at— you just told me backstage, it can’t be regulated.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Well, you have this growth in these companies that’s just extraordinary. And I think it is likely that you will see an attempt to regulate them a lot more. The competition is that— I think the technology businesses that would be regulated are much more complex and much more difficult to understand and figure out how to even write the right legislation to regulate them with.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">And so I happen to be a pessimist. I know the E.U. is going to try. Whether the U.S. government does or not, I don’t know, but I just don’t think it’s as easy as it sounds. And yet, they continue to get larger and larger.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">And I’m not editorializing about that. I think, with size sometimes comes abuse. And you could argue that now, there are different ways that size creates abuse. What is— this is Matt King. What is your perspective on the metaverse, NFTs, and other emerging technologies and their potential impact on entertainment media? And call it a metaverse. I don’t think there’ll be one metaverse.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">I think— I talk about democratization. It’ll be dispersed. You may have an avatar, but you’ll go all over the place. And I think that it’s likely to be developed into something real as an experience.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">That said, there’s been enough said and criticized about toxic behavior in internet 2.0— Twitter, Facebook, you name it. And I think it’s something that has to be considered.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Do you think Instagram should make Instagram for kids?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">No. No, but it’s not my business.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Hmm.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I do think— NFTs, I think, are real. I think the ability to collect things, even if they’re digital— we forget, in our generation, that things don’t have to be physical.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">They can be digital, and they have meaning to people. And as long as that meaning can be essentially substantiated in a blockchain, I think you’re going to see an explosion of things being created, traded, collected in NFTs.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Now, Disney has not gone into this area as heavily as—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Disney has. Disney has done some licensing. I’m not—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Of things.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I don’t have all the details, because it happened at the very end of my watch. But there is some— look, when you think about all the copyright and trademarks, characters Disney has—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Exactly.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">—and the NFT possibilities, they’re extraordinary.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">They can also be stolen. There’s been a lot of fraud around that.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">And copy— the other thing I’ve noticed— I went on a platform called OpenSea, which is a platform to buy and trade NFTs.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Mm-hmm.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">And I was amazed at all the Disney stuff that was there, and most of it was pirated.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yes.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Most of it was not created by people who had the right to create them.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Well, it’s like early YouTube, if you recall all those lawsuits.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Very much so. Everybody always says, what’s your favorite? I always say, Tinkerbell. I don’t know why that was so easy. And then, they say, well, who would you like to be? And I would say, Thor, but that was because Chris Hemsworth is just a god, right?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Mm-hmm.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Who wouldn’t want to look like that?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Right. Do you relate?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I don’t know if I’d relate. I think I’m probably more of a Woody than a Buzz. I don’t know.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">What have you found to be the superpower that you possess that has led to your success? And I want you to actually tell me what superpower you would like.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I have— I don’t know whether this is my parents’ doing or I was born this way or both, but I have an incredible work ethic. I just work hard, and it’s served me incredibly well. And right next to that, which I know was drawn out by my parents, is an insatiable curiosity to just learn more and more.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">OK. What about an actual superpower?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Like what? I don’t run fast, or— what?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Something. Hello, you make Marvel movies. There’s like— come on.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Those are fantasies.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah, I know. What is your fantasy superpower?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Oh, what’s my fantasy.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah, I’m not saying you’re going to get it. I’ll start— invisibility. Go ahead.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">No, no, no.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Totally. Are you kidding? I’d totally sneak around.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I don’t have an ambition to be a superhero.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I know, but what superpower—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">And so I don’t feel I need superpowers.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">None? Didn’t you guys just make the “Eternals“? I mean, come on. Which one would you like?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">To live forever.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Live forever? Which one do you want? None of them. You want no superpower. All right. I’m gonna be invisible.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I’d like to be— I’d like other things. I’d love to be able to paint.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Paint? Oh, there you go. That’s not a superpower. You can’t do that.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">When I retired, George Bush sent me a note saying, what are you going to do now? Paint? ‘Cause he paints.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Do you own a George Bush? No.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">He sent me the book.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Oh, no. OK. What criticism of Disney Company has hit you the hardest?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Well, I take any criticism about how we’ve treated our people very— I took it very personally. That would be it.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">So you have a specific one?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Well, the main one is wages. But I’ve defended that before, because we were one of the first companies to go to $10 an hour. We went to $15 an hour, minimum wage. In both cases, they were higher than state and federal minimums at the time.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">You know, when you manage these companies, you’re balancing multiple forces when it comes to finances. And this is not an excuse, but you need to grow your profits for your shareholders. And maybe I’m talking to some government officials right now about— this is for them. You need to do that.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">You need to keep your pricing reasonable for consumers in mind, which in an inflationary environment, just gets even more critical. And you’d like to pay your people whatever it takes to make them happy and to keep them motivated, but they all kind of have to fit in with one another in some very careful balance.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yeah.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Because if— I don’t know anybody who wouldn’t want to pay their people more, but then you got to figure out, well, if we do that, and you have to grow your bottom line, can you raise prices? And then, you alienate your consumers. And so it’s a delicate balance.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Just saying, maybe you could pay C.E.O.s less. OK. Who— [LAUGHTER]</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">I’m teasing.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">That— I— yeah. I knew you were going there.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">You walked right into it. You just literally walked right into it. OK. Last question. Can you sum up your legacy in five words or less?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I was going to say I can’t—</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Ah! That’s not five words.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I came, I saw, I conquered, is six. Five words or less— oh, I’ll just say one thing. I was fortunate enough to be kind of handed the keys to the kingdom, to get the opportunity to run a company that had an incredible reputation and was important in the world. And I wanted to make sure when I left, that I burnished that legacy, that it was stronger, more admired, better than it was when I got it. I feel that I’ve accomplished that.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">All right, but five words. That wasn’t five.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">I can’t.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Yes, you can. Five words. No?</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">No, no. I was a force— I don’t know. May the force be with you.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Oh, there you go. Thank you, Bob Iger.</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">bob iger</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">Thank you.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">[MUSIC PLAYING]</p></dd><dt class="css-xx7kwh">kara swisher</dt><dd class="css-4gvq6l"><p class="css-8hvvyd">“Sway” is a production of New York Times Opinion. It’s produced by Nayeema Raza, Blakeney Schick, Daphne Chen, Caitlin O’Keefe, and Wyatt Orme, edited by Nayeema Raza, with original music by Isaac Jones, mixing by Isaac Jones and Carole Sabouraud, and fact-checking by Kate Sinclair and Mary Marge Locker. Special thanks to Shannon Busta, Kristin Lin, and Kristina Samulewski. And thanks also to Thomas Vecchione, Nicholas Seaver and the team at the Richmond Forum, where we taped this event.</p><p class="css-8hvvyd">Irene Noguchi is the executive producer of New York Times Opinion Audio. If you’re in a podcast app already, you know how to get your podcasts, so follow this one. If you’re listening on the Times website and want to get each new episode of “Sway” delivered to you along with that magical churro smell— disgusting— download any podcast app, then search for “Sway,” and follow the show. We release every Monday and Thursday. Thanks for listening.</p></dd></dl></div></div></div></div><div style="position:absolute;width:0;height:0;visibility:hidden;display:none"></div><header class="css-1vwfk9f" data-breakpoint=""><div
